model.label: MiMo-V2-Pro
model.description: MiMo • The flagship foundation for the agentic era, built for complex, long-horizon, multi-step agentic tasks.

pick.rationale: Duke holds the #1 overall ranking with elite metrics on both ends of the floor (KenPom/Torvik rank #1, offense rank 4, defense rank 1), but the absence of starting point guard Caleb Foster (foot fracture) and center Patrick Ngongba II significantly narrows their advantage against a Michigan team with nearly identical elite metrics (rank #2, offense rank 7-8, defense rank 2). Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg is expected to play through his low-ankle sprain, giving the Wolverines a healthier roster for this championship matchup. While Duke's superior efficiency margin (39.17 vs 38.38) and Cameron Boozer's star power provide a slight edge, Michigan's balanced attack, defensive prowess, and full-strength lineup make this essentially a coin-flip game between two championship-caliber programs.

reasoningStep.title: National Championship: Duke vs Michigan
reasoningStep.summary: Extremely close matchup between two elite teams with nearly identical analytical profiles, but Duke's significant injuries to key starters tip the scales slightly toward Michigan's healthier roster.
evidence: Duke: KenPom rank 1, Torvik rank 1, 31-2 record, adj_em 39.17, offense rank 4, defense rank 1
Michigan: KenPom rank 2, Torvik rank 2, 31-2 record, adj_em 38.38, offense rank 7-8, defense rank 2
Duke missing Caleb Foster (foot fracture) and Patrick Ngongba II - two starters out
Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg dealing with low-ankle sprain but expected to play
Duke has won 11 straight games including ACC Tournament without Foster/Ngongba
Both teams entered tournament as #1 seeds with identical 31-2 records
Michigan slightly healthier roster gives them marginal advantage in this coin-flip matchup

toolCalls.summary: Fetched https://wolverineswire.usatoday.com/story/sports/college/wolverines/mens-basketball/2026/03/18/yaxel-lendeborg-injury-update-for-ncaa-tournament/89219691007/ and extracted 2253 characters of text.

result.text: Yaxel Lendeborg injury update for NCAA Tournament Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Recruiting Big Ten Sports Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg ready for NCAA Tournament despite injury Isaiah Hole Wolverines Wire Near the end of the Big Ten Tournament championship game, while not the worst news in the world, it wasn't good news for the maize and blue: star forward Yaxel Lendeborg got rolled up on and ended the loss to Purdue with a low-ankle sprain. Timing-wise, it's not ideal for the Michigan basketball star, but at the same time, it could be worse, as he appears to be able to go in the NCAA Tournament, which starts on Tuesday. On Wednesday, as the Wolverines prepare to take on Howard in the Round of 64, Lendeborg should be ready to go, but at the same time, he isn't entering the tournament at 100%. "Yeah, I’m fine. I started moving, running around yesterday," Lendeborg said. "I had a little bit of issues cutting a little bit, but today I think I’m going to feel a lot better. "When I get into the game mindset, I think I’ll be all right." Having come from junior college and then UAB, this is the first time that Lendeborg is playing for a favorite in the NCAA Tournament. Because he got that earlier experience, he knows what it's like to be in Howard's shoes. But now that he is where he is, he's determined to suit up and do everything he can to ensure that the season goes on as long as it possibly can. "Everybody is a threat. For them to get here, they have to win the championship," Lendeborg said. "I’ve been in that predicament before when I was a mid major. We’re going to go in there with nothing to lose so we’re going to play our hearts out. And no matter what happens, we’re happy we made it. "Being on this side now, it’s the same thing Rez said, I don’t want to lose, and this is going to be my last college game ever, so I want to continue my streak." The Wolverines and Bison will tip off at 7:10 p.m. EDT, and the game will be broadcast on CBS. Timing-wise, it's not ideal for the Michigan basketball star, but at the same time, it could be worse, as he appears to be able to go in the NCAA Tournament, which starts on Tuesday. On Wednesday, as the Wolverines prepare to take on Howard in the Round of 64, Lendeborg should be ready to go, but at the same time, he isn't entering the tournament at 100%. "Yeah, I’m fine. I started moving, running around yesterday," Lendeborg said. "I had a little bit of issues cutting a little bit, but today I think I’m going to feel a lot better. "When I get into the game mindset, I think I’ll be all right." Having come from junior college and then UAB, this is the first time that Lendeborg is playing for a favorite in the NCAA Tournament. Because he got that earlier experience, he knows what it's like to be in Howard's shoes. But now that he is where he is, he's determined to suit up and do everything he can to ensure that the season goes on as long as it possibly can. "Everybody is a threat. For them to get here, they have to win the championship," Lendeborg said. "I’ve been in that predicament before when I was a mid major. We’re going to go in there with nothing to lose so we’re going to play our hearts out. And no matter what happens, we’re happy we made it.
